body_txt: In many places, such as militaries, hospitals and some schools, people wear uniforms. The purpose of a uniform is to create... uniformity ... by making everyone look equal and the same. However, in artistic character design, one of the basic principles is that all important characters should be immediately distinguishable from each other . Hence, characters who are meant to stand out will wear a "uniform" that is different from everyone else. This is separate from rank-based outfits although it may be implied that they're too badass for anyone to complain. This is not when there's a practical reason to be dressed differently such as fireproof clothing for a pyrokinetic character. When somebody sympathetic is doing this and getting away with it, it's almost certainly a symptom of Mildly Military . In a non-mild military, it's a way of showing the character's contempt for regulations — probably marking them as an enormous asshole who's too connected to discipline, unless the army deserves contempt. This may also be a reason for most cop shows to center on plainclothes detectives instead of uniform officers, as even high-ranking brass on the detective side only wear uniforms under specific circumstances. On the other hand, detectives in media tend to select "plain clothes" with enough similarities to be uniform, making them easily recognizable before they identify themselves, and justifying enough customization to reflect character traits and investigative style. Compare Transfer Student Uniforms , where the character still stands out, but from wearing the uniform of a previous school or station, and Bling of War where a military uniform is impractically fancy. Also compare Non-Uniform Uniform , for modifications to a uniform that's still technically within the uniform's guidelines. For a specific variety of Custom Uniform where the differences are intended to point up the wearer's attractiveness, see Custom Uniform of Sexy . This trope is a common source of Flashy Protagonists, Bland Extras .
